content: We can hypothesize that the neural synchrony-based working memory retention mechanism from long-horizon active learning can resolve the 2025 ICM curiosity module's core limitation of 'curiosity drift' in open-ended long-horizon autonomous agent tasks, by preserving sparse, distant reward-relevant unexpected observations in activated long-term memory to sustain structured curiosity over extended task horizons. This synergistic integration would allow curious autonomous agents to avoid getting stuck cycling through trivial, low-information novel distractions by prioritizing retention of novel observations that connect to distant, high-reward goals.
source_topics: Working memory retention mechanisms for long-horizon active learning
trigger_topic: Working memory retention mechanisms for long-horizon active learning
